examples.title: Singly Linked List Node Structure
examples.description: Basic self-referential structure definition for singly linked list
examples.explanation: This code defines a self-referential structure where 'struct Node *next' points to another struct Node. The malloc allocates heap memory, arrow operator accesses members through pointer, and NULL marks end of list.

examples.title: Insert Node at Beginning of Linked List
examples.description: Function to add a new node at the start of the list
examples.explanation: The insertAtBeginning function: allocates new node, assigns data, makes new node point to current head, then updates head to new node. This creates nodes in reverse order of insertion. Display traverses until NULL.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for insertion, O(n) for display

examples.title: Delete Node from Linked List
examples.description: Function to delete a node with specific value from linked list
examples.explanation: Delete function handles three cases: 1) Empty list returns NULL, 2) Delete head updates head pointer and frees old head, 3) Delete middle/find uses two pointers (prev and current) to maintain link after deletion. FREE is crucial to prevent memory leaks.

examples.title: Doubly Linked List Structure
examples.description: Self-referential structure with both next and previous pointers
examples.explanation: Doubly linked list has two self-referential pointers: prev and next. Each node maintains bidirectional links enabling traversal in both directions. When inserting, both prev and next pointers must be updated correctly to maintain the doubly link structure.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for insertion at end, O(n) for traversal
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for operations, O(n) for storage
